cards.text: What are the stages of haemostasis?
cards.answer: 1. Vascular spasm (immediate vasoconstriction). 2. Primary haemostasis: platelet adhesion (via vWF), activation, and aggregation forming platelet plug. 3. Secondary haemostasis: coagulation cascade (intrinsic + extrinsic -> common pathway) forming fibrin clot. 4. Fibrinolysis: plasmin dissolves clot.

tip.tip: Four stages from vessel injury to clot dissolution.

cards.text: What is the difference between iron deficiency and megaloblastic anaemia?
cards.answer: Iron deficiency: microcytic hypochromic, low ferritin, high TIBC, target cells. Megaloblastic: macrocytic, B12 or folate deficiency, hypersegmented neutrophils, megaloblasts in marrow. B12 deficiency also causes neurological signs (subacute combined degeneration).

tip.tip: Small red cells vs large red cells.

cards.text: What is the Rh system and why does it matter?
cards.answer: Rh D antigen on RBCs. Rh-negative individuals develop anti-D antibodies after exposure to Rh-positive blood. Critical in pregnancy: Rh-negative mother with Rh-positive fetus can develop antibodies causing haemolytic disease of newborn (erythroblastosis fetalis). Prevented by anti-D immunoglobulin.

cards.text: What are the functions of plasma proteins?
cards.answer: Albumin: oncotic pressure, transport. Globulins: alpha/beta (transport), gamma (antibodies/immunoglobulins). Fibrinogen: coagulation. Total protein: 6-8 g/dL. A/G ratio normally >1. Low albumin causes oedema.

tip.tip: Albumin is the most abundant plasma protein.

cards.answer: Pathological activation of coagulation throughout the vasculature. Causes widespread microthrombi AND bleeding (consumption of clotting factors and platelets). Triggered by sepsis, obstetric emergencies, malignancy. Labs: low platelets, low fibrinogen, high D-dimer, prolonged PT/APTT.

cards.text: What is the ESR and what does it indicate?
cards.answer: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: rate at which RBCs settle in a tube. Increased by fibrinogen, immunoglobulins (cause rouleaux formation). Elevated in infection, inflammation, malignancy, pregnancy. Non-specific marker. Normal: <15 mm/hr (males), <20 mm/hr (females).

cards.text: What are the types of white blood cells?
cards.answer: Granulocytes: neutrophils (60-70%, phagocytosis), eosinophils (2-4%, parasites/allergy), basophils (0-1%, histamine). Agranulocytes: lymphocytes (20-30%, T and B cells, immunity), monocytes (3-8%, become macrophages).

tip.tip: Never Let Monkeys Eat Bananas (decreasing frequency).

cards.answer: Autosomal recessive: HbS (glutamic acid -> valine at position 6 of beta chain). HbS polymerises under low O2, causing sickling. Leads to vaso-occlusive crises, chronic haemolysis, organ damage. Heterozygotes (trait) have malaria protection.

tip.tip: Single amino acid change with devastating consequences.
